title: What I owe the person who kept the water running
summary: Reflect on what we owe to the workers who maintain essential infrastructure at night, and argue whether public recognition matters or only pay does.
topic: What we owe to the people who keep essential services running at night
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (400–500 words) reflecting on the workers who maintain essential services while most people sleep — water treatment, power lines, overnight road repair, hospital boilers. Choose one specific role and describe what their night shift involves. Then argue whether public recognition of their work matters, or whether fair pay is sufficient. Use present perfect, a conditional, and 'although' or 'however' at least once each.
hintStarters: Most of us have never given much thought to where clean water comes from at three in the morning, but…
Although a fair wage is the most practical form of recognition, I would argue that…
If the operators walked off the job tonight, the city would begin to notice by…

hintVocab.meaningVi: cơ sở hạ tầng thiết yếu
giám sát mức độ hóa chất
phản ứng với một sự cố
sự công nhận của công chúng
coi điều gì đó là hiển nhiên
ngăn chặn một cuộc khủng hoảng
kéo dài hơn ca làm việc

hintVocab.phrase: the background hum of the city
hintVocab.meaningVi: tiếng ồn âm trầm nền của thành phố
cảm thấy được nhìn nhận, được chú ý đến

sampleText:
At two in the morning, an operator at the Dhaka water treatment plant is watching a bank of screens showing flow rates, chlorine levels, and pump pressures across ninety kilometres of pipeline. The overnight team has been doing this seven days a week for decades. Their job is not glamorous: it involves running diagnostics, adjusting dosing pumps, and calling out maintenance crews when a fault appears on the grid. If they do it well, nothing happens. The city wakes up, opens its taps, and water comes out. That invisibility is the whole point, and it is also, in a quiet way, the problem.

What this particular worker — and the thousands like them across the world — has never received public acknowledgement of any kind. Their names do not appear in the news. No one thanks them in the morning. The argument that fair pay is sufficient recognition is reasonable, but I think it misses something. Pay compensates a worker for their time. It does not tell them that the city understands what it depends on, or that the population values the skill they have spent years developing. There is a difference between being paid and being seen. Several cities in Brazil and Colombia have introduced small annual ceremonies for infrastructure workers, not as a substitute for fair wages but as a way of naming the relationship between the people who maintain the city and the people who live in it. Although these ceremonies are modest, the operators interviewed afterwards consistently described them as meaningful — not because of the event itself, but because of the simple act of being named.

However, I would not want to argue that recognition alone is enough. A worker who is publicly praised but underpaid has received a hollow gesture. The order matters: first, wages that reflect the responsibility and the unsociable hours; then, if the city is serious, some form of acknowledgement that reaches the people who do this work before dawn. If every resident could see, even once, exactly what it takes to keep water flowing and power stable through the night, the political pressure for fair pay would probably look after itself. The invisibility of essential work is not just a social problem. It is a fiscal one. When people do not understand what a service costs to maintain, they are easily persuaded to cut its budget. Visibility is not just a kindness to workers. It is a prerequisite for informed public decisions.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Present perfect continuous to describe long-running work
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'The overnight team has been doing this seven days a week for decades' dùng hiện tại hoàn thành tiếp diễn (has been + V-ing + for) để nhấn mạnh hoạt động không gián đoạn trong một thời gian rất dài, vẫn đang tiếp diễn đến hiện tại.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'The overnight team has been doing this seven days a week for decades' uses the present perfect continuous (has been + -ing + for) to emphasise an unbroken, ongoing activity over a very long period that is still continuing now.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: First conditional to describe a real, predictable consequence
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'If they do it well, nothing happens' dùng điều kiện loại 1 (if + hiện tại đơn, hiện tại đơn) để mô tả mối quan hệ nhân quả thực tế và có thể đoán trước — đây là loại công việc mà thành công nghĩa là không có gì xảy ra.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'If they do it well, nothing happens' uses a first conditional (if + present simple, present simple) to describe a real, predictable causal relationship — this is the kind of work where success means nothing happens.

sampleTranslationVi:
Lúc hai giờ sáng, một vận hành viên tại nhà máy xử lý nước Dhaka đang nhìn vào một dãy màn hình hiển thị lưu lượng, mức clo và áp suất bơm trên chín mươi cây số đường ống. Nhóm ca đêm đã làm điều này bảy ngày một tuần trong nhiều thập kỷ. Công việc của họ không hào nhoáng: bao gồm chạy kiểm tra chẩn đoán, điều chỉnh bơm định lượng và gọi các nhóm bảo trì khi sự cố xuất hiện trên mạng lưới. Nếu họ làm tốt, không có gì xảy ra. Thành phố thức dậy, mở vòi nước, và nước chảy ra. Sự vô hình đó chính là toàn bộ vấn đề.

Những gì người công nhân này — và hàng nghìn người giống họ trên khắp thế giới — chưa bao giờ nhận được là sự công nhận của công chúng. Tên của họ không xuất hiện trên tin tức. Không ai cảm ơn họ vào buổi sáng. Lập luận rằng mức lương công bằng là sự công nhận đủ là hợp lý, nhưng tôi nghĩ nó bỏ lỡ điều gì đó. Lương bù đắp cho người lao động về thời gian của họ. Nó không cho họ biết rằng thành phố hiểu nó phụ thuộc vào điều gì, hay rằng dân cư trân trọng kỹ năng mà họ đã dành nhiều năm phát triển. Có sự khác biệt giữa được trả lương và được nhìn nhận. Một số thành phố ở Brazil và Colombia đã đưa ra các buổi lễ thường niên nhỏ cho các công nhân cơ sở hạ tầng, không phải là sự thay thế cho mức lương công bằng mà là cách đặt tên cho mối quan hệ giữa những người duy trì thành phố và những người sống trong đó. Mặc dù các buổi lễ này khiêm tốn, các vận hành viên được phỏng vấn sau đó đã nhất quán mô tả chúng là có ý nghĩa — không phải vì bản thân sự kiện, mà vì hành động đơn giản được gọi tên.

Tuy nhiên, tôi không muốn lập luận rằng sự công nhận một mình là đủ. Một công nhân được khen ngợi công khai nhưng bị trả lương thấp đã nhận được một cử chỉ rỗng tuếch. Thứ tự quan trọng: đầu tiên, mức lương phản ánh trách nhiệm và giờ làm việc bất thường; sau đó, nếu thành phố nghiêm túc, một hình thức công nhận nào đó đến được với những người làm công việc này trước bình minh. Nếu mỗi cư dân có thể thấy, dù chỉ một lần, chính xác những gì cần thiết để giữ cho nước chảy và điện ổn định qua đêm, áp lực chính trị để có mức lương công bằng có lẽ sẽ tự lo liệu. Sự vô hình của công việc thiết yếu không chỉ là vấn đề xã hội. Đó là một vấn đề tài chính.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the present perfect continuous correctly to describe long-running work?
options: The overnight team has been work here for decades.
The overnight team is working here for decades.
The overnight team has been working here for decades.
The overnight team has worked here since decades.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect continuous = has/have been + -ing + for + duration. 'Has been work' uses the base form. 'Is working … for decades' is present continuous, which does not describe a situation starting in the past. 'Has worked since decades' uses the wrong preposition — 'for' is used with a period of time, 'since' with a point in time.
mcQuestions.hint: Present perfect continuous = has/have been + -ing. 'For' introduces a duration, 'since' a starting point.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses a first conditional to describe a predictable outcome?
options: If they will do their job well, nothing visible happens.
If they do their job well, nothing visible will happen.
If they did their job well, nothing visible happens.
If they do their job well, nothing visible happens.
mcQuestions.explanation: First conditional (general/habitual): if + present simple, present simple. This describes a universal causal relationship. Using 'will' in the if-clause is non-standard. 'Will happen' shifts to a predictive future, not a general truth. 'Did' is second conditional.
mcQuestions.hint: For a general, habitual truth (not a specific future event), both clauses use present simple.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'although' to concede and then counter an argument?
options: Although these ceremonies are modest, operators describe them as meaningful.
Although these ceremonies are modest but operators describe them as meaningful.
These ceremonies are modest, although operators describe them as meaningful.
Although these ceremonies are modest, operators describes them as meaningful.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Although + clause, + main clause' — no 'but'. The last option has a subject-verb agreement error: 'operators' is plural, so 'describe' (not 'describes') is correct. Option C reverses the rhetorical direction.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Although' concedes; no 'but' after the comma. Check subject-verb agreement in the main clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'there is a difference between' with two gerunds?
options: There is a difference between to be paid and to be seen.
There is a difference between being paid and being seen.
There is a difference between paid and seen.
There is a difference among being paid and being seen.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Between … and' takes parallel forms. After 'between', gerunds ('being paid') are standard. 'To be paid … to be seen' uses infinitives, which are less natural here. 'Paid … seen' are adjectives/past participles without the gerund structure. 'Among' is used for more than two items.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Between X and Y' takes parallel forms — gerunds are natural here.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase most naturally expresses that recognition without fair pay is meaningless?
options: Public praise without fair pay is a void gesture.
Public praise without fair pay is a empty gesture.
Public praise without fair pay is a hollow gesture.
Public praise without fair pay is an hollow gesture.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'A hollow gesture' is the standard expression for something superficial or insincere. 'A void gesture' is not idiomatic in this context. 'A empty' uses the wrong article — 'empty' starts with a vowel sound, so 'an' is needed. 'An hollow' is wrong because 'hollow' starts with a consonant sound.
mcQuestions.hint: There is a fixed expression for a gesture that is meaningless because it lacks substance.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'the skill they have spent years developing'?
options: They value the skill which they have spend years developing.
They value the skill they have been spent years developing.
They value the skill they have spent years to develop.
They value the skill they have spent years developing.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Spend time doing something' uses a gerund. 'Have spent … developing' is present perfect + gerund. 'Have spend' is non-standard. 'Have been spent' is passive, which changes the meaning. 'Spent years to develop' uses an infinitive incorrectly after 'spend time'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Spend time doing' = spend + time + gerund. Check the form after 'spent years'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'first … then' to sequence two steps?
options: First, fair wages; then, public acknowledgement.
At first, fair wages; then, public acknowledgement.
Firstly, fair wages; after, public acknowledgement.
First off, fair wages; afterwards, public acknowledgement.
mcQuestions.explanation: In listing a sequence of priorities, 'first … then' is the natural pair. 'At first' means 'initially' and implies a change later. 'Firstly … after' mixes a formal adverb with an informal one. 'First off … afterwards' is too informal for this argumentative register.
mcQuestions.hint: 'First … then' sequences priorities. 'At first' implies a contrast or change, which is not the meaning here.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses a modal with 'look after itself'?
options: The political pressure for fair pay would probably look itself after.
The political pressure for fair pay would probably look after itself.
The political pressure for fair pay would probably look for itself.
The political pressure for fair pay would probably look into itself.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Look after itself' = take care of itself, resolve on its own. The reflexive object follows the particle: 'look after itself'. 'Look itself after' inverts the order incorrectly. 'Look for itself' = search for itself (different meaning). 'Look into itself' = investigate itself (different meaning).
mcQuestions.hint: The phrasal verb 'look after something' puts the object after the particle.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence most naturally describes the idea that a job involves being present and responsive?
options: They are in call throughout the night in case a fault appears.
They are at call throughout the night in case a fault appears.
They are on call throughout the night in case a fault appears.
They are on calling throughout the night in case a fault appears.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'On call' is the fixed expression for being available and ready to respond. 'In call' and 'at call' are not standard. 'On calling' is not a phrase.
mcQuestions.hint: Only one preposition collocates with 'call' to describe being available to respond at any time.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'not … but' to clarify a distinction?
options: They received the award not as a substitute for pay but as a form of recognition.
They received the award not as a substitute to pay, but as a form of recognition.
They received the award not as a substitution for pay, but as a form of recognition.
They received the award not as a substitute for pay, but as a form of recognition.
mcQuestions.explanation: Both options A and B are grammatically correct, but option A uses a comma before 'but' for clarity in a complex sentence, which is the more common practice in formal writing. 'Substitute to' uses the wrong preposition — it should be 'substitute for'. 'Substitution' is a noun from a different grammatical context.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Substitute for' is the correct collocation. A comma before 'but' in a long 'not X but Y' structure aids clarity.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'it is a … one' to refer back to a previously mentioned type of problem?
options: The invisibility of essential work is not just a social problem — it is a fiscal one.
The invisibility of essential work is not just a social problem — it is also a fiscal.
The invisibility of essential work is not just a social problem — it is a fiscally one.
The invisibility of essential work is not just a social problem — it is one fiscal.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'A fiscal one' uses 'one' as a pronoun substitute for 'problem'. 'Also a fiscal' drops the pronoun. 'A fiscally one' uses an adverb where an adjective is needed. 'One fiscal' reverses the adjective-noun order incorrectly.
mcQuestions.hint: 'One' can substitute for a previously mentioned noun. The adjective must come before 'one'.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The sample suggests that public recognition matters, but that pay should come first. Do you think these two things are connected — would better recognition lead to better pay, or are they entirely separate issues? Can you give an example from your own context?
